evidence: goseg/protocol/contracts/protocol_contracts.go and goseg/protocol/contracts/startram_contracts.go each define file-local contract constants plus separate catalog-entry builder functions (`protocolContractCatalogEntries`, `startramContractCatalogEntries`) with repeated manual registration structure.

Both files encode boundary-critical identifiers/messages as independent literals (for example `ContractID` strings and descriptor names/descriptions) without an explicit shared mechanism in these files to enforce cross-catalog consistency, uniqueness, or registration completeness.
No import-time side effects were observed in either file; coupling risk is from distributed manual registry construction rather than runtime initialization behavior.

Large function-pointer dependency bags are repeated across modules: `importerRuntime` defines 24 callback fields plus extensive nil-guard plumbing and pass-through methods (`goseg/importer/importer.go:79-327`), while orchestration defines many Fn-suffixed seam structs and bundle builders (`goseg/docker/orchestration/runtime_ops.go:15-365`).

Public API pairs repeatedly wrap `default*Runtime()` and immediately forward to `*ForRuntime` variants without adding policy (for example `ListWifiSSIDs`/`listSSIDsForRuntime`, `ConnectToWifi`/`connectToWifiForRuntime` in `goseg/system/wifi.go:328-373`, and `OpenUploadEndpoint`/`openUploadEndpointForRuntime`, `validateUploadRequest`/`validateUploadRequestForRuntime` in `goseg/importer/importer.go:381-550`).

Ship config persistence is implemented as six near-identical adapters with per-type copy/mutate/write logic (`goseg/shipworkflow/operations_runtime.go:289-402`), while orchestration runtime seams expose separate per-type updater callbacks (`goseg/docker/orchestration/runtime_ops.go:64-75`), reinforcing duplication instead of a single sub-config mutation abstraction.

Version/container defaults are maintained in two independent representations: structured literals in `goseg/defaults/defaults.go:67-110` and a large JSON blob in `goseg/defaults/version.go:15-187`, duplicating repo/tag/hash sources.

Multiple modules implement repeated wrapper chains (public function -> *ForRuntime helper -> service/runtime method) with near-pass-through behavior, e.g. orchestration wrappers in `goseg/docker/orchestration/orchestration.go` and WiFi wrappers in `goseg/system/wifi.go`.

State/protocol handlers combine decoding, auth/session mutation, transport dispatch, and timeout/concurrency logic in one unit (`goseg/leak/handler.go`), and ship transition builders combine config mutation + container lifecycle branching in single closures (`goseg/shipworkflow/urbit_operations_runtime.go`).

Config patching is centralized in a very large registry with repeated parse/has/apply closures for many keys (`goseg/config/config_update_schema.go`), increasing structural drift risk and making behavioral changes high-friction.

findings.summary: Contract boundary definitions are fragmented across per-file manual catalog builders, increasing drift risk as modules evolve.
evidence: `protocolContractCatalogEntries()` and `startramContractCatalogEntries()` each hand-build `[]contractCatalogEntry` with duplicated schema fields (`Name`, metadata, compatibility), but no visible shared enforcement point in these files.

Boundary identifiers (`ContractID`, action tokens, error message constants) are declared independently by concern area, so additions/renames can diverge in structure/semantics without a single architectural contract source.
findings.suggestion: Introduce a single contracts-registry construction path (or schema-driven/code-generated source) that all concern-specific definitions feed into, and enforce invariants (unique IDs, required metadata, naming/namespace rules) via centralized validation tests to keep cross-module boundaries coherent.

findings.summary: Callback-bag seam pattern adds heavy indirection with little behavioral leverage
evidence: `importerRuntime` holds many callbacks and then re-wraps them in one-line methods plus nil checks (`goseg/importer/importer.go:79-327`).
`runtime_ops` defines many Fn-based seam structs and default bundle assembly that mostly wires globals into fields (`goseg/docker/orchestration/runtime_ops.go:15-365`).
`RectifyRuntime` repeats the same seam-and-forward pattern (`goseg/rectify/rectify.go:29-109`).
findings.suggestion: Replace broad callback bags with a small set of capability interfaces per workflow (for example import lifecycle, container state, startram sync), inject them only at orchestration boundaries, and remove one-line forwarding methods that only nil-check then delegate.

findings.summary: Default-wrapper plus ForRuntime dual APIs create low-value call-depth across modules
evidence: WiFi exports mostly call `defaultWiFiRuntime()` and immediately delegate (`goseg/system/wifi.go:328-373`).
Importer repeatedly exposes entrypoints that only call `*ForRuntime(..., defaultImporterRuntime())` (`goseg/importer/importer.go:381-550`).
Ship workflow repeatedly constructs default runtime in thin wrappers before delegating (`goseg/shipworkflow/operations_runtime.go:266-389`).
findings.suggestion: Standardize on one public API style: either explicit dependency injection at top-level constructors or package-level defaults internally. Remove mirrored `X`/`XForRuntime` pairs unless both variants carry distinct policy.

findings.summary: Per-subconfig persistence helpers are copy-pasted instead of using one generic abstraction
evidence: `persistShipRuntimeConfigWith`, `persistShipNetworkConfigWith`, `persistShipFeatureConfigWith`, `persistShipWebConfigWith`, `persistShipScheduleConfigWith`, and `persistShipBackupConfigWith` all repeat the same fallback algorithm with only type/field substitutions (`goseg/shipworkflow/operations_runtime.go:289-402`).
Runtime seams expose one callback per subconfig type (`goseg/docker/orchestration/runtime_ops.go:64-75`), which encourages continued duplication at call sites.
findings.suggestion: Introduce a single typed helper (for example `persistSubconfig[T]`) parameterized by getter/setter closures and optional specialized persister, then map all subconfig updates through that one abstraction.

findings.summary: Two independent default-version representations create abstraction drift risk
evidence: `defaults.go` hardcodes repo/tag/hash defaults in typed structs (`goseg/defaults/defaults.go:67-110`).
`version.go` also hardcodes repo/tag/hash data in `DefaultVersionText` JSON and unmarshals it at init (`goseg/defaults/version.go:15-187`).
findings.suggestion: Consolidate default version/container metadata into one canonical source (typed model or JSON asset), then generate/derive other forms from it to eliminate manual dual maintenance.

findings.summary: Contract governance is documented centrally but not encoded as enforceable per-contract evolution policy in the contract declaration modules.
evidence: README.md requires protocol/error compatibility descriptors and references shared active/deprecated helpers as part of governance.

protocol_contracts.go defines all contract catalog entries with identical metadata (DefaultContractIntroducedIn + CompatibilityBackwardSafe), so compatibility intent is effectively implicit/default rather than explicit per contract.
startram_contracts.go follows the same default-only metadata pattern, so contract evolution policy is not locally expressed where contracts are defined.
findings.suggestion: Introduce an explicit contract lifecycle declaration convention in the contract files (for example, required per-entry compatibility/lifecycle fields and optional deprecation metadata), and add a CI assertion that rejects new/changed contracts without explicit lifecycle metadata.

findings.summary: StarTram error contract exports raw message text, encouraging cross-module coupling to presentation strings instead of stable contract IDs.
evidence: startram_contracts.go exports APIConnectionErrorMessage as a package-level untyped string constant.
The same file already defines APIConnectionError as a typed ContractID and attaches message text in the descriptor, indicating identity and text are separate concerns.
README.md governance emphasizes typed contract APIs for protocol/error surfaces, but exported raw message constants permit downstream callers to depend directly on text-level semantics.
findings.suggestion: Make message text non-exported (or expose only typed accessors tied to ContractID), and require cross-module consumers to resolve messages through contract descriptors/accessor functions rather than importing string constants.

findings.summary: Repeated pass-through runtime wrapper layers create systemic structural duplication and blur the real ownership boundary.
evidence: `orchestration.go` repeatedly defines package-level methods that only validate/forward to runtime methods, while runtime methods mostly forward again to lifecycle/network calls (e.g., lines 83-103, 116-142, 174-208).
`wifi.go` repeats exported -> `...ForRuntime` -> `newWiFiService(runtime)` forwarding for most operations (lines 328-374), adding ceremony without distinct policy boundaries.
`session.go` has a large set of runtime methods that perform nil-guard boilerplate and directly pass through to state.logstream methods (lines 222-316).
`collectors.go` duplicates default-runtime and explicit-runtime entrypoints for the same workflows (e.g., lines 437-477 and 516-547), increasing API surface with little behavioral differentiation.
findings.suggestion: Collapse to a single explicit seam pattern per subsystem: keep either package facade or runtime object (not both), centralize validation once, and generate or template repetitive runtime adapters where dual entrypoints are still required for tests.

findings.summary: Handler modules mix protocol decoding, auth/session mutation, and dispatch side effects, producing multi-responsibility flows that are hard to evolve safely.
evidence: `leak/handler.go` combines payload decoding (`decodePacketPayload`), protocol typing/routing (`routeAction`), auth state mutation (`urbitLogin`/`urbitLogout`), channel dispatch (`sendToLeakChannel`), and timeout goroutine behavior (`watchPasswordLogoutTimeout`) in one file.

`shipworkflow/urbit_operations_runtime.go` transition builders embed both domain policy and infrastructure actions inside step closures (e.g., `buildTogglePowerSteps` lines 309-363 and `buildToggleNetworkSteps` lines 389-429).
`auth/auth.go` also carries session/runtime wiring and token authorization behavior together, reinforcing the same boundary blur between session state access and request/auth policy.
findings.suggestion: Split by responsibility into protocol adapters, domain transition services, and transport/state gateways; keep handlers thin orchestration points that call focused components with explicit interfaces.

findings.summary: Config mutation schema is concentrated in a monolithic registry with repetitive closure triplets, making change behavior inconsistent and hard to reason about.
evidence: `config_update_schema.go` keeps a very large `confPatchRegistry` (starting line 139) where each key repeats parse/has/apply boilerplate with mostly mechanical variation.
The same file also owns parsing utilities and registry build/init logic (lines 936-1067), so schema declaration, parse strategy, and execution policy are tightly coupled.
This file size/shape (1000+ LOC) creates high blast radius for small field additions and encourages copy-paste divergence in update semantics.
findings.suggestion: Move to declarative field specs (typed parser + target accessor/setter) and compose update behavior from reusable primitives; split registry declaration from parser helpers and execution engine into separate files.

findings.summary: Operational modules combine unrelated concerns (system mutation, diagnostics, and output formatting), reducing cohesion and increasing side-effect coupling.
evidence: `disk.go` handles filesystem creation/mount/fstab reconciliation, multipart temp cleanup, and SMART health policy in one module (e.g., lines 198-270, 282-354, 385-481).
`logger.go` mixes logger bootstrap/state management with container log retrieval and JSON response shaping (`getDockerLogs` and `RetrieveSysLogHistory`, lines 312-377).
`system/system.go` similarly mixes host metrics collection with fixer-script/cron orchestration, reinforcing broad utility-style modules instead of focused services.
findings.suggestion: Split modules by stable responsibility (e.g., disk provisioning vs disk health checks; logger initialization vs log-history transport formatting) and expose small composable services per concern.
